howItWorks.intro: Getting pasta portions right avoids waste and ensures everyone is satisfied. The key is knowing dry-to-cooked expansion ratios, which vary by pasta shape.
rules: Main course: 2 oz (56g) dry pasta per person is standard
Side dish: 1-1.5 oz (28-42g) dry pasta per person
Dry pasta roughly doubles in weight when cooked (some shapes expand more)
Use 4-6 quarts of water per pound of dry pasta for proper cooking
Add ½ cup sauce per person as a starting point for sauced dishes
howItWorks.outro: These portions are based on standard USDA serving sizes and real-world cooking experience. Hungry adults may eat 3-4 oz dry, while children need only 1-1.5 oz. Adjust based on how saucy and rich your dish is.

faq.question: How much dry pasta is one serving?
faq.answer: One serving of dry pasta is 2 oz (56g) for a main course. That's about the diameter of a quarter when you bundle long pasta like spaghetti, or about 1 cup of short pasta like penne. It yields roughly 1 cup cooked.

faq.question: How much does pasta expand when cooked?
faq.answer: Most dry pasta roughly doubles in weight and volume when cooked. Spaghetti doubles, penne and rigatoni expand about 2x, while orzo and small shapes can expand 2.5-3x. Fresh pasta (ravioli, tortellini) expands only slightly.

faq.question: How much water do I need to cook pasta?
faq.answer: Use 4-6 quarts (1-1.5 gallons) of water per pound of pasta. Plenty of water prevents sticking, maintains a rolling boil, and dilutes starch so the pasta cooks evenly. Salt the water generously — about 1 tablespoon per quart.

faq.question: How much sauce per serving of pasta?
faq.answer: A good rule is ½ cup (4 oz) of sauce per 2 oz serving of dry pasta. Rich, thick sauces (Alfredo, Bolognese) need less. Light sauces (aglio e olio, marinara) may need a bit more. The pasta should be coated, not swimming.

faq.question: How do I measure spaghetti without a scale?
faq.answer: Bundle dry spaghetti and measure the diameter: a quarter-sized bundle (about 1 inch) is roughly 2 oz — one serving. A nickel-sized bundle is about 1 oz. For short pasta, 1 cup dry is approximately 2 oz.

faq.question: Should I cook extra pasta?
faq.answer: Cook 10-15% more than you calculate, especially for groups. Pasta is cheap, and having a little extra is better than running short. Leftover cooked pasta keeps 3-5 days in the fridge and reheats well.
